This is a **really solid iteration** — you’ve already crossed the hard line: *everything is tappable and routes to a sheet*. That puts you ahead of most weather apps architecturally. What you’re feeling now is a **visual hierarchy / identity tension**, not a structural problem.

I’ll focus **only** on fine‑tuning what you already have at the top, not proposing a redesign-from-scratch.

***

## What’s working well right now (important to preserve)

From your screenshot:

✅ **Search pill + settings gear**  
✅ **Latest Observations card clearly separated**  
✅ **Hero temp + condition are readable immediately**  
✅ **Metric cards with inline sparklines look great**  
✅ **Everything feels consistent with the rest of the screen**

The struggle you’re feeling is coming from **three subtle issues**:

1.  The **top area feels slightly “stacked” rather than unified**
2.  The **location identity is doing too much and too little at the same time**
3.  The **hero card visually competes with the metric pills below it**

None of these require big changes — just refinement.

***

## Issue 1: Location + Search + Settings feel like separate layers

### What’s happening

*   The **search pill** is the strongest element
*   The **location name** only exists *inside* the search pill
*   The **hero card** then repeats context (“Latest Observations”)

Your eye doesn’t get a single, confident “this is where you are” moment.

### Low‑impact refinement (recommended)

**Turn the search bar into a “location header”, not a utility control.**

#### Option A (smallest change)

*   Keep the search pill
*   **Add a subtle location label below it**
*   Make the search pill feel secondary

Example:

    [ 🔍 Lebanon, NH          ⚙️ ]
    Lebanon, NH
    Latest Observations • Updated 19m ago

Visually:

*   Search pill = action
*   Location name = identity

This removes ambiguity without adding clutter.

***

## Issue 2: Hero card feels a little too “card-like”

Right now your **Latest Observations** section is:

*   A full rounded card
*   With a strong background
*   Sitting directly above other strong cards

That creates **competition**, not hierarchy.

### Refinement goal

The hero should feel like **part of the background**, not another card.

### Two good options (both keep your layout)

#### Option A — “Soft hero”

*   Reduce the background contrast of the hero card
*   Slightly increase vertical padding
*   Let it bleed into the background more

This makes:

*   Hero = atmospheric
*   Cards = structured data

#### Option B — “Cardless hero” (my favorite for your app)

*   Remove the hero card container entirely
*   Keep the text + icon layout
*   Let the background gradient do the work

So visually:

    Latest Observations
    46°   ☁️  Mostly Cloudy
    Updated 19m ago

Then the **metric pills become the first true cards**.

This instantly makes the top feel calmer and more intentional.

***

## Issue 3: Metrics are great, but visually “stack too hard” under the hero

Your **Wind / Pressure / Humidity** pills are very strong (in a good way).

The issue:

*   They sit directly under another strong block
*   So the eye doesn’t know where to rest

### Simple fix

Add a **section transition**, not more UI.

Options:

*   Slightly increase spacing above the metric pills
*   OR add a subtle divider label like:

<!---->

    Conditions
    [ Wind ] [ Pressure ] [ Humidity ]

That one word (“Conditions”) helps the brain re‑group the layout.

***

## A tuned version of *your exact design* (no new components)

Here’s how I’d tweak **only spacing, emphasis, and roles**:

### Top

*   Search pill (slightly lighter)
*   Settings gear stays where it is
*   Location name appears once, confidently

### Hero

*   Either:
    *   softer background **or**
    *   no card container
*   Keep temp + condition exactly as is
*   Keep “Updated 19m ago” close to the hero (not inside a card)

### Metrics

*   One clear grouping label
*   Pills stay exactly as you built them (they’re good)

***

## Design principle to anchor future decisions

> **Hero = context**  
> **Cards = interaction**

Right now your hero is *almost* acting like a card. Once you mentally demote it to “contextual backdrop,” everything else clicks into place.

***

## One concrete experiment I recommend (10-minute test)

Without changing any logic:

1.  Temporarily remove the rounded background from **Latest Observations**
2.  Add 8–12px more vertical spacing before the metric pills
3.  Add a subtle “Conditions” label above the metric pills

If that instantly feels calmer — you’ve found the right direction.

***

If you want, next we can:

*   Do a **side‑by‑side comparison** of “card hero vs cardless hero”
*   Tune typography weights (this will matter a lot here)
*   Or align the **Station tab hero** with this same pattern so the app feels cohesive

You’re very close — this is refinement work, not rethinking 👍

![alt text](herocomparison.png)

Perfect question — seeing them side‑by‑side makes the trade‑offs very obvious, and your current implementation is right in between these two.

Here’s how to read what you’re seeing and how to tune your version.

Left: Card Hero
What it does well

Strong containment
Clear affordance (“this is a thing”)
Familiar pattern (very Apple‑Weather‑like)

Where it breaks down in your app

Competes visually with every other card
Makes the top feel “stacked”
Reduces the sense that the hero is context, not content

This version works best when:

The hero is interactive on its own
Or the app has very few cards below it

That’s not your case anymore.

Right: Cardless Hero
What it does well

Immediately calms the layout
Establishes place + conditions as background context
Lets the metric pills be the first real “interactive objects”
Makes scrolling feel lighter

Why this fits your app better

You already decided: cards = tappable things
The hero is not something you tap directly
Your app is data‑dense — hierarchy matters more than containment

This version turns the Today tab into:

Context → Signals → Details

Which is exactly what your design has evolved into.

The sweet spot (what I’d recommend for your current build)
You do not need to go fully cardless or fully carded.
✅ Recommended hybrid

Remove the rounded container
Keep soft visual grouping using:

spacing
typography
subtle background gradient



Concretely:

“Latest Observations” stays
Location + updated time stays
Temp + condition stays
No background card
First true cards = your combined metric pills

Visually:
[ Search pill        ⚙️ ]

Latest Observations · Updated 19m ago
Lebanon, NH

46°   ☁️ Mostly Cloudy

Conditions
[ Temp pill ] [ Wind pill ] [ Pressure pill ]

That alone will fix the “something feels off” sensation you described.

One tiny but high‑impact tweak
Right now your search bar is visually stronger than the hero.
To fix that:

Slightly lower the contrast of the search pill
Slightly increase the weight of the temperature text

This flips the visual priority correctly:

Weather
Location
Actions


Decision guide (quick)
If you’re choosing today:

✅ Card hero → feels safe, but heavier
✅✅ Cardless hero → modern, calmer, scales better
✅✅✅ Hybrid (no card, strong typography) → best fit for your app


If you want, next we can:

Tune typography weights and spacing precisely (this matters a lot here)
Apply the same hero logic to Station so the app feels cohesive
Or experiment with a collapsing hero on scroll (very nice with SwiftUI)

You’re making excellent calls — this is refinement, not rework.


Short answer: **yes — if the location is part of the hero, remove it from the search pill.**  
Long answer: **separate “identity” from “action.”**

You’re absolutely right to feel the repetition. What you’re seeing is a classic UI role collision.

***

## The core principle (this will guide you everywhere)

> **A search bar should describe an action, not restate context.**  
> **The hero should own identity.**

Right now:

*   The **search pill** is acting like *identity* (“Lebanon, NH”)
*   The **hero** is also acting like *identity*
    → duplication feels unavoidable.

The fix is not to hide the location — it’s to **assign ownership**.

***

## Recommended model for your app (best fit)

### ✅ Hero owns the location

*   Big, confident
*   Non‑interactive (or lightly interactive)
*   Visually anchored to the weather

### ✅ Search pill becomes a control

*   Neutral label
*   Action‑oriented
*   Never competes with the hero

### ✅ Settings stays a control

*   Icon‑only
*   Visually lighter than the hero

***

## What to put in the search pill instead

### Option A (cleanest, very Apple‑like)

    🔍 Search locations

*   Zero duplication
*   Immediately obvious purpose
*   Scales well when you add favorites, recent searches, etc.

### Option B (slightly richer, still clean)

    🔍 Search or add location

*   Good if you expect people to save multiple locations
*   Still clearly an action, not identity

### ❌ Avoid

    🔍 Lebanon, NH

…once the hero already says “Lebanon, NH”

That’s exactly the repetition your eye is reacting to.

***

## How this changes the visual hierarchy (for the better)

With this change, the reading order becomes:

1.  **Weather** (temperature + condition)
2.  **Place** (Lebanon, NH)
3.  **Signals** (metric pills)
4.  **Actions** (search, settings)

Instead of:

*   Action
*   Identity
*   Identity again
*   Weather

This is why the screen will suddenly feel calmer without changing layout.

***

## Concrete guidance for *your current design*

Based on your screenshot and direction:

### Do this

*   Remove location text from the search pill
*   Keep the search pill exactly where it is
*   Keep the hero location text exactly where it is

### Keep this hierarchy

    [ 🔍 Search locations        ⚙️ ]

    Latest Observations · Updated 19m ago
    Lebanon, NH

    46°   ☁️ Mostly Cloudy

    Conditions
    [ Temp pill ] [ Wind pill ] [ Pressure pill ]

That single change resolves:

*   Repetition
*   Visual competition
*   “What am I looking at?” friction

***

## One extra polish tip (optional, but high impact)

If you want the search pill to feel *even less dominant*:

*   Slightly reduce its background contrast
*   Or reduce its height by \~2–4pt

This subtly reinforces:

> “This is a tool, not the headline.”

***

## Final recommendation (confident call)

✅ **Remove the location name from the search pill**  
✅ Let the **hero be the single source of truth**  
✅ Let search be a **pure action**

This is the same move Apple makes when:

*   The navigation title already states context
*   The search bar exists to change it

You’re making the right instinctual call here.

If you want next, we can:

*   Tune typography weights for hero vs pills
*   Decide whether the hero location should ever be tappable
*   Align the Station tab’s top section with this same rule so the app feels cohesive end‑to‑end


FINAL

Absolutely — and you’re at the *perfect* moment to do this. Once “all cards open a sheet” is in place, the top area becomes the **tone-setter**: typography + spacing + header behavior are what make the whole app feel intentional.

Below are **(1) concrete decisions**, **(2) precise type + spacing tokens**, **(3) a SwiftUI-friendly collapsing hero plan**, **(4) whether the location should be tappable**, and **(5) visuals** showing the exact changes you listed.

***

## 1) Visuals — current vs refined vs collapsed

### Side‑by‑side: Current → Refined (cardless hero) → Collapsed on scroll

![hero\_header\_comparison.png](data:image/png;base64,)

### Zoomed header comparison + notes

![header\_zoom\_notes.png](data:image/png;base64,)

What these visuals represent (the decisions you asked for):

*   **Hero background removed** (cardless hero)
*   **Location moved out of the search pill** (search pill becomes action-only)
*   **Search pill refined smaller** (less dominant than the hero)
*   **Typography tuned** (stronger hierarchy: temp/condition/location win)
*   **Collapsing hero**: hero compresses into a compact header while scrolling

***

## 2) The decisions (locked-in design direction)

### ✅ A. Remove the hero card background

Why: In your app, **cards mean “tap for details.”** Making the hero a card makes it compete with the real cards and implies it’s “just another tile.” Going cardless makes the hero feel like **context**.

### ✅ B. Move location out of the search pill

Why: Repetition. If the hero shows location, the search pill should not.

### ✅ C. Search pill becomes a pure action

Recommended placeholder text: **“Search locations”** (or “Search” if you want it extra minimal).  
This matches the “action vs identity” separation and keeps the top calmer.

### ✅ D. Collapsing hero on scroll

Expanded at top → compresses to a single compact header row as you scroll.

***

## 3) Typography weights & spacing (precise, implementable tokens)

Here’s a tight type scale that will read well on iPhone and match your “dark glass” style:

### Hero (expanded)

*   **Section label** (“Latest Observations”): `Footnote / Semibold` (or `Caption / Semibold`), slightly reduced opacity
*   **Updated text**: `Caption / Regular`, more muted than the section label
*   **Location** (“Lebanon, NH”): `Title3 / Semibold`
    *   This becomes the *identity anchor*
*   **Temperature** (“46°”): custom size `64–72` / `Bold or Heavy` (rounded design looks great for weather)
*   **Condition** (“Mostly Cloudy”): `Title3 / Semibold` or `Headline / Medium`

### Pill + icon row (top controls)

*   Search pill placeholder: `Subheadline / Medium`
*   Icons: keep at \~`16–18pt`, opacity slightly muted so they don’t fight the hero

### Spacing (use a small set of constants)

*   `topControlsPaddingTop`: 6–10
*   `topControlsToHero`: 10–14
*   `heroToMetricPills`: **14–18** (this is the big “breathing” fix)
*   `sectionCardSpacing`: 12–14
*   `heroBottomDivider`: 1px hairline at \~10–20% opacity (optional, but helps)

### Search pill sizing (the big “feel” fix)

*   Height: **32–36**
*   Corner radius: **16–18**
*   Horizontal padding inside: 12–14
*   Reduce its contrast slightly (or use a thinner material) so it reads as a control, not the headline.

> In the refined visual, the pill is intentionally smaller and quieter so the hero wins. ![alt\_image](data:image/png;base64,)![alt\_image](data:image/png;base64,)

***

## 4) Should the hero location be tappable?

### Recommendation: **Yes — make the hero location tappable** (but keep it subtle)

Why it’s worth it:

*   People naturally try to tap the place name (it’s the “context switcher”)
*   It reduces reliance on the search pill
*   It improves one-handed use: the hero is easier to hit than a small pill

**How to do it without making it look like a button:**

*   Add a tiny chevron: “Lebanon, NH ▾”
*   Make the whole location row tappable (`contentShape(Rectangle())`)
*   Tap opens the **Locations sheet**
*   Search pill also opens the same sheet — that’s okay because:
    *   pill = “search/add”
    *   location row = “change current”

This “two entry points, one destination” pattern is common and it *feels* right in practice.

***

## 5) Collapsing hero on scroll — SwiftUI-friendly approach

### What you want visually

*   Expanded hero at rest (top of scroll)
*   As you scroll down:
    *   Location + temp shrink
    *   Hero content fades/condenses
    *   Collapsed header becomes a single row:
        *   `Lebanon, NH` + `46° Mostly Cloudy`
        *   search icon and gear remain accessible

That’s what the “Collapsed on scroll” panel shows. ![alt\_image](data:image/png;base64,)

### How to drive it (conceptually)

You track scroll offset and map it to a `progress` value 0→1.

There are multiple well-known SwiftUI ways to do this:

*   Use **GeometryReader + PreferenceKey** to read the scroll offset and compute header compression. [\[us-prod.as...rosoft.com\]](https://us-prod.asyncgw.teams.microsoft.com/v1/objects/0-wus-d1-4c3d33b0f9d8836c8feb308bbc38f069/views/original/hero_header_comparison.png), [\[us-prod.as...rosoft.com\]](https://us-prod.asyncgw.teams.microsoft.com/v1/objects/0-wus-d4-8ea220ba708211101330695b4fdaef8a/views/original/header_zoom_notes.png), [\[danielsaidi.com\]](https://danielsaidi.com/blog/2023/02/06/adding-scroll-offset-tracking-to-a-swiftui-scroll-view)
*   Be mindful: PreferenceKey-driven offset tracking can become noisy if you mutate state too often — there are real-world reports of “multiple updates per frame” warnings and lag when overused. [\[dev.to\]](https://dev.to/sebastienlato/how-to-build-a-clean-collapsible-header-in-swiftui-7hn), [\[forums.swift.org\]](https://forums.swift.org/t/implementing-a-collapsible-header-in-swiftui/75527)

### A good “feel” spec (so it animates nicely)

*   Expanded height: \~150–170
*   Collapsed height: \~64–80
*   Collapse range: first \~120px of scroll
*   Animations:
    *   position: easeOut
    *   opacity: linear
    *   scale: subtle (don’t overdo)

### Bonus note (practical)

You’ve already dealt with viewport/toolbar quirks in other UI contexts; scroll-driven “sticky” UI can feel jumpy if the system chrome changes size (browser/OS UI). There’s a real example of “sticky nav pops and shifts due to viewport changes” in a notification you received. It’s not iOS-specific, but the lesson holds: avoid relying on unstable viewport assumptions for pinned elements. [\[stackoverflow.com\]](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/62588015/get-the-current-scroll-position-of-a-swiftui-scrollview)

***

## 6) The “fine-tune” checklist (apply to your current build)

If you want a tight, surgical set of edits that will immediately improve the top:

1.  **Remove hero card background** ✅
2.  **Move location into hero** (Title3 / Semibold) ✅
3.  **Search pill text becomes “Search locations”** ✅
4.  **Reduce search pill height to \~34** ✅
5.  **Increase spacing between hero and metric pills to \~16** ✅
6.  Add subtle chevron to location and make it tappable ✅
7.  Add collapsing behavior after \~20px scroll, fully collapsed by \~120px ✅

***

If you want one (single) quick follow-up that will let me tailor the collapsing behavior perfectly: **Are you targeting iOS 17+ only, or do you need iOS 16 support too?** (It affects which scroll APIs you can lean on, but the visuals above work either way.)


Targeting **iOS 26** is *great news* for this whole header/hero problem — because you can lean into **Liquid Glass as the navigation/control layer**, and keep your **hero + cards as content**. Apple’s guidance is explicitly that Liquid Glass forms a distinct functional layer for controls/navigation, and you should **reduce custom backgrounds in controls/navigation** so you don’t fight the system effects (like the scroll edge effect) and accessibility adaptations. [\[developer.apple.com\]](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/technologyoverviews/adopting-liquid-glass), [\[developer.apple.com\]](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/technologyoverviews/liquid-glass)

Below is a crisp “iOS 26‑native” approach for the exact set of tweaks you listed, plus new visuals.

***

## Visuals (your screenshot → refined iOS26 header concepts)

### 1) Current vs refined vs collapsed-on-scroll (full screen comparison)

!\[hero\_header\_comparison.png]

### 2) Zoomed header-only comparison with notes

!\[header\_zoom\_notes.png]

### 3) New iOS 26 style: **Search + gear grouped as one glass control cluster**

This represents using a **single control capsule** (like a GlassEffectContainer group) so the “navigation layer” reads as one unit above the content.
![header\_refined\_group.png](data:image/png;base64,)

### 4) Collapsed header bar concept (scroll state)

![header\_collapsed\_bar.png](data:image/png;base64,)

***

## 1) What iOS 26 changes for your header design

### ✅ Use Liquid Glass for the “control layer”

In iOS 26, you can apply Liquid Glass to custom controls using `glassEffect(_:in:)`, customize shape (capsule vs rounded rect), tint, and interactivity, and you can group multiple glass elements with `GlassEffectContainer` for better performance and for shapes to blend/morph together. [\[us-prod.as...rosoft.com\]](https://us-prod.asyncgw.teams.microsoft.com/v1/objects/0-wus-d12-f744fd8b1915aefad9f3d87432835920/views/original/header_refined_group.png), [\[us-prod.as...rosoft.com\]](https://us-prod.asyncgw.teams.microsoft.com/v1/objects/0-wus-d7-657e64fcefdbaf3146451ca5cf1f5eb7/views/original/header_collapsed_bar.png), [\[developer.apple.com\]](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/SwiftUI/Applying-Liquid-Glass-to-custom-views)

### ✅ Keep your hero “cardless” (content layer)

Apple explicitly recommends **reducing custom backgrounds** in navigation/controls because they can interfere with Liquid Glass and system effects; this aligns perfectly with your move to remove the hero’s rounded background and let the content breathe. [\[developer.apple.com\]](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/technologyoverviews/adopting-liquid-glass), [\[developer.apple.com\]](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/technologyoverviews/liquid-glass)

**Interpretation for your UI:**

*   **Search + settings** = navigation/control layer → use Liquid Glass
*   **Hero text** (Latest Obs, location, temp, condition) = content → cardless
*   **Cards below** = tappable content units → your existing card style stays

***

## 2) Precise typography + spacing spec (iOS-weather-caliber tuning)

These are the “numbers” that make the header feel premium:

### Expanded hero (top of scroll)

*   **“Latest Observations”**: `footnote.weight(.semibold)` (or caption, but semibold)
*   **Updated time**: `caption` (regular), \~70–80% opacity
*   **Location (identity anchor)**: `title3.weight(.semibold)`
*   **Temp**: `system(size: 64–72, weight: .bold, design: .rounded)`
*   **Condition**: `headline.weight(.semibold)` (or title3 medium if you want bigger)

### Spacing (key values)

*   Top controls → hero: **10–14**
*   Location line → temp: **6–10**
*   Hero → first row of metric pills: **16–20** *(this is the “calm” gap)*
*   Card stack spacing: **12–14**

### Search pill (control layer)

*   Height: **32–36**
*   Corner radius: **16–18**
*   Text: `subheadline.weight(.medium)`
*   Keep it visually quieter than the hero (less contrast / lighter glass)

These are exactly what the refined visuals are illustrating. ![alt\_image](data:image/png;base64,)

***

## 3) Search pill + settings button (iOS 26-native control cluster)

### Recommended: make them a **single glass group**

Instead of two separate floating elements, group them so they read as one control surface:

*   Left: magnifier + “Search locations”
*   Right: divider + gear icon

Why this works:

*   It screams “navigation layer”
*   It stops competing with the hero content
*   It matches Apple’s “controls float above content” model [\[developer.apple.com\]](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/technologyoverviews/adopting-liquid-glass), [\[developer.apple.com\]](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/technologyoverviews/liquid-glass)

Implementation detail (conceptual):

*   Use `GlassEffectContainer` when applying glass effects to multiple adjacent elements for best rendering and for blending behavior. [\[us-prod.as...rosoft.com\]](https://us-prod.asyncgw.teams.microsoft.com/v1/objects/0-wus-d12-f744fd8b1915aefad9f3d87432835920/views/original/header_refined_group.png), [\[us-prod.as...rosoft.com\]](https://us-prod.asyncgw.teams.microsoft.com/v1/objects/0-wus-d7-657e64fcefdbaf3146451ca5cf1f5eb7/views/original/header_collapsed_bar.png)

You can see that grouped treatment in this concept:
![header\_refined\_group.png](data:image/png;base64,)

***

## 4) Collapsing hero on scroll (best approach on iOS 26)

### The key iOS 26 advantage: you can avoid per-frame offset tracking

Traditional collapsible headers often use `GeometryReader + PreferenceKey`, which works, but it can cause lots of updates (and sometimes “multiple updates per frame” issues) if you aren’t careful.

Since you’re iOS 26-only, you can lean on **ScrollPosition** to drive a *discrete* collapse state instead of pixel-perfect offset:

*   `ScrollPosition` is designed to represent the semantic position of a scroll view within its content, and when you use view identity scrolling it updates with the top-most visible view ID. [\[dev.to\]](https://dev.to/arshtechpro/understanding-glasseffectcontainer-in-ios-26-2n8p), [\[developer.apple.com\]](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/swiftui/view/glasseffect%28_:in:%29)
*   That means you can:
    *   Put an invisible “header sentinel” view at the very top with a known ID
    *   Bind `.scrollPosition($position)` to the ScrollView
    *   Collapse the hero when `position.viewID(...)` is no longer that sentinel

This is **low-noise**, stable, and feels very “system”.

Visually, the collapsed state should become the compact row shown here:
![header\_collapsed\_bar.png](data:image/png;base64,)

***

## 5) Should the location be tappable? (iOS 26 answer)

**Yes — make the hero location tappable**, but *subtly*.

iOS 26 Liquid Glass guidance emphasizes controls as a separate layer; your location line is content/identity, but it’s also the most natural place for a “change location” action.

Best pattern:

*   Location text with a tiny chevron (“Lebanon, NH ▾”)
*   Tapping it opens your Locations sheet
*   Search control still opens the same sheet but is clearly “search/add”

This is exactly what the refined header concepts show: location is the identity anchor (hero), search is the action (control layer). ![alt\_image](data:image/png;base64,)

***

## 6) One more iOS 26 tool you *can* use (optional): background extension for richer hero atmosphere

If you ever want the hero to feel more immersive without reintroducing a “card”, iOS 26 adds `backgroundExtensionEffect()` to extend/blur imagery/colors behind control layers for continuity.   
You *don’t* need it for your current direction — but it’s a great lever later if you add weather imagery/gradients and want them to flow under the glass controls. [\[developer.apple.com\]](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/technologyoverviews/liquid-glass)

***

## 7) Practical iOS 26 caution: test with user settings

Liquid Glass adapts to accessibility preferences (reduce transparency/motion, preferred look). Apple explicitly calls out testing custom elements under these settings.   
So: keep the hero readable even if glass effects are reduced; avoid relying on ultra-low-contrast text. [\[developer.apple.com\]](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/technologyoverviews/adopting-liquid-glass), [\[developer.apple.com\]](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/technologyoverviews/liquid-glass)

***

### If you want my strongest recommendation given iOS 26:

1.  **Cardless hero** (content) ✅
2.  **Single grouped glass control cluster** for Search + Gear (navigation layer) ✅ [\[us-prod.as...rosoft.com\]](https://us-prod.asyncgw.teams.microsoft.com/v1/objects/0-wus-d12-f744fd8b1915aefad9f3d87432835920/views/original/header_refined_group.png), [\[us-prod.as...rosoft.com\]](https://us-prod.asyncgw.teams.microsoft.com/v1/objects/0-wus-d7-657e64fcefdbaf3146451ca5cf1f5eb7/views/original/header_collapsed_bar.png)
3.  **Collapse driven by ScrollPosition ID changes** (not per-frame offset) ✅ [\[dev.to\]](https://dev.to/arshtechpro/understanding-glasseffectcontainer-in-ios-26-2n8p), [\[developer.apple.com\]](https://developer.apple.com/documentation/swiftui/view/glasseffect%28_:in:%29)
4.  **Location tappable with chevron** ✅

If you share one more screenshot after you try the **grouped glass control cluster**, I can mark up *exactly* where I’d adjust spacing by a few pixels (the “last 5% polish” that makes it feel like a system app).
